texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yuhan Wang + , Hua Jin + , Qiang Ma, Kai Mo, Haizhuo Mao, Armin Feldhoff, Xingzhong Cao, Yanshuo Li,* Fusheng Pan,* and Zhongyi Jiang*
texts.text: Yuhan Wang + , Hua Jin + , Qiang Ma, Kai Mo, Haizhuo Mao, Armin Feldhoff, Xingzhong Cao, Yanshuo Li,* Fusheng Pan,* and Zhongyi Jiang*

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract: Metal-organic framework (MOF) glasses are promising candidates for membrane fabrication due to their significant porosity, the ease of processing, and most notably, the potential to eliminate the grain boundary that is unavoidable for polycrystalline MOF membranes. Herein, we developed a ZIF-62 MOF glass membrane and exploited its intrinsic gas-separation properties. The MOF glass membrane was fabricated by melt-quenching treatment of an in situ solvothermally synthesized polycrystalline ZIF-62 MOF membrane on a porous ceramic alumina support. The molten ZIF-62 phase penetrated into the nanopores of the support and eliminated the formation of intercrystalline defects in the resultant glass membrane. The molecular sieving ability of the MOFmembrane is remarkably enhanced via vitrification. The separation factors of the MOF glass membrane for H2/CH4, CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 mixtures are 50.7, 34.5, and 36.6, respectively, far exceeding the Robeson upper bounds.
texts.text: Abstract: Metal-organic framework (MOF) glasses are promising candidates for membrane fabrication due to their significant porosity, the ease of processing, and most notably, the potential to eliminate the grain boundary that is unavoidable for polycrystalline MOF membranes. Herein, we developed a ZIF-62 MOF glass membrane and exploited its intrinsic gas-separation properties. The MOF glass membrane was fabricated by melt-quenching treatment of an in situ solvothermally synthesized polycrystalline ZIF-62 MOF membrane on a porous ceramic alumina support. The molten ZIF-62 phase penetrated into the nanopores of the support and eliminated the formation of intercrystalline defects in the resultant glass membrane. The molecular sieving ability of the MOFmembrane is remarkably enhanced via vitrification. The separation factors of the MOF glass membrane for H2/CH4, CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 mixtures are 50.7, 34.5, and 36.6, respectively, far exceeding the Robeson upper bounds.

texts.orig: M embrane-based separation technologies are playing a critical role in the purification and separation of chemicals due to their low energy consumption and environmental friendliness relative to conventional separation processes. [1] Microporous inorganic materials that exhibit molecular sieving properties, with zeolites as a representative, have become promising candidates for the fabrication of highperformance membranes. In the last decade, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have become a hotspot of research in membrane materials because they display unique structural and chemical versatility compared to zeolites. [2] Nevertheless, similar to zeolite membranes, the polycrystalline nature of MOF membranes poses the greatest challenge for their

texts.orig: fabrication and utilization, that is the presence of defects and unavoidable grain boundaries. [3] As a matter of fact, the sieving effect is so sensitive that very few defects in the membrane would compromise the selectivity significantly. Many researchers have long been engaged in reducing the grain boundary defects and cracks, and accordingly various post-treatment methods, for example, coking deposition [4] and chemical vapor deposition modification, [5] have been developed. Despite the improved performance achieved, the fundamental problem of grain boundaries remains unsolved.
texts.text: fabrication and utilization, that is the presence of defects and unavoidable grain boundaries. [3] As a matter of fact, the sieving effect is so sensitive that very few defects in the membrane would compromise the selectivity significantly. Many researchers have long been engaged in reducing the grain boundary defects and cracks, and accordingly various post-treatment methods, for example, coking deposition [4] and chemical vapor deposition modification, [5] have been developed. Despite the improved performance achieved, the fundamental problem of grain boundaries remains unsolved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MOF glasses as a newly developed family of MOF materials have been steadily capturing scientific interest. [6] MOF glasses are generally formed via solid-liquid transition followed by the quenching of the corresponding liquid MOFs. Bennett et al first reported the thermally induced melting of ZIF-4 under an argon atmosphere and the resulting meltquenched ZIF-4 glass after cooling of the liquid. [7] The ZIF-4 liquid retained short-range order (i.e. chemical configuration and coordinative bonding) after melting, though with a clear loss of long-range order. Turning to the microscopic mechanism, Zn-N linkages partially break at high temperature and the resultant undercoordinated zinc ions have the potential to link with another neighboring imidazole group for exchange. [8] A number of MOFs including ZIF-62, ZIF-76, and TIF-4 have also been shown to undergo melting prior to decomposition, excitingly expanding the family of MOF glasses. As a summary of the reports so far, MOF glasses possess the following fascinating features: 1) The formation of the liquid state has the distinct advantage of easy processability. 2) The well-maintained porosity potentially offers unique opportunities for separation. 3) Structural tunability and composition diversity offer a broad design space (i.e., aside from pure MOF glasses, various complex hybrid glasses have also been developed). [9] Therefore, MOF glass materials are highly likely to afford the prospective for generating high-performance membranes without grain boundary structures. However, there has been no report on the MOF glass-based membranes till now. Herein, for the first time, we report the design and fabrication of a MOF glass membrane, as well as the exploration of its intrinsic gas separation properties.
texts.text: MOF glasses as a newly developed family of MOF materials have been steadily capturing scientific interest. [6] MOF glasses are generally formed via solid-liquid transition followed by the quenching of the corresponding liquid MOFs. Bennett et al first reported the thermally induced melting of ZIF-4 under an argon atmosphere and the resulting meltquenched ZIF-4 glass after cooling of the liquid. [7] The ZIF-4 liquid retained short-range order (i.e. chemical configuration and coordinative bonding) after melting, though with a clear loss of long-range order. Turning to the microscopic mechanism, Zn-N linkages partially break at high temperature and the resultant undercoordinated zinc ions have the potential to link with another neighboring imidazole group for exchange. [8] A number of MOFs including ZIF-62, ZIF-76, and TIF-4 have also been shown to undergo melting prior to decomposition, excitingly expanding the family of MOF glasses. As a summary of the reports so far, MOF glasses possess the following fascinating features: 1) The formation of the liquid state has the distinct advantage of easy processability. 2) The well-maintained porosity potentially offers unique opportunities for separation. 3) Structural tunability and composition diversity offer a broad design space (i.e., aside from pure MOF glasses, various complex hybrid glasses have also been developed). [9] Therefore, MOF glass materials are highly likely to afford the prospective for generating high-performance membranes without grain boundary structures. However, there has been no report on the MOF glass-based membranes till now. Herein, for the first time, we report the design and fabrication of a MOF glass membrane, as well as the exploration of its intrinsic gas separation properties.

texts.orig: ZIF-62 is a mixed-linker framework of nominal composition Zn(Im)1.75 (Bim)0.25 in which Zn 2 + is coordinated to imidazole (Im) and benzimidazole (Bim) (Figure 1a). [10] ZIF62 is analogous to the well-studied ZIF-4, which has the same framework architecture and space group. In contrast, ZIF-62 displays a lower melting temperature than ZIF-4, along with a much wider melting range. Moreover, ZIF-62 undergoes only one melting process before decomposition, not interfering with intermediate recrystallization. Given its ultrahigh

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A systematic investigation of the structure, pore size distribution, chemical composition, and adsorption properties of ZIF-62 glass was carried out before we fabricated glass membranes. Firstly, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiments were carried out under an inert argon atmosphere (Figure 1c). A broad endothermic peak at ca. 200300 8 C corresponded to the solvent release. The ZIF-62 crystals melted at 434 8 C, consistent with prior observations. [11] The melting temperature ( T m) of ZIF-62 crystals can be engineered by adjusting the molar ratio of Im to Bim. [12] Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis (Figure S1) exhibited no obvious weight loss during the whole melting process, suggesting the excellent thermal stability of ZIF-62. The NMR spectrum (Figure 1e) was in good accordance with the TG analysis, demonstrating that the chemical bonding and components are basically unchanged by the heating process. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Figure S2) of the assynthesized ZIF-62 showed a high degree of crystallinity, and all peaks matched well with the reported phase of ZIF-62. The Bragg diffraction peaks of ZIF-62 glass disappeared, which was indicative of the distortion of typical crystalline structure.
texts.text: A systematic investigation of the structure, pore size distribution, chemical composition, and adsorption properties of ZIF-62 glass was carried out before we fabricated glass membranes. Firstly, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiments were carried out under an inert argon atmosphere (Figure 1c). A broad endothermic peak at ca. 200300 8 C corresponded to the solvent release. The ZIF-62 crystals melted at 434 8 C, consistent with prior observations. [11] The melting temperature ( T m) of ZIF-62 crystals can be engineered by adjusting the molar ratio of Im to Bim. [12] Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis (Figure S1) exhibited no obvious weight loss during the whole melting process, suggesting the excellent thermal stability of ZIF-62. The NMR spectrum (Figure 1e) was in good accordance with the TG analysis, demonstrating that the chemical bonding and components are basically unchanged by the heating process. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Figure S2) of the assynthesized ZIF-62 showed a high degree of crystallinity, and all peaks matched well with the reported phase of ZIF-62. The Bragg diffraction peaks of ZIF-62 glass disappeared, which was indicative of the distortion of typical crystalline structure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: structure factors, provided the atom-atom distance and relational bond information. The PDFs of ZIF-62 crystal and glass were near identical for distances up to 8 . In detail, the D ( r ) curves displayed the expected peaks at interatomic distances of 1.5 , 2 , 3 , 4 , and 6 , corresponding to C � C/C � N, Zn � N1, Zn � C, Zn � N2, and Zn � Zn pair correlations, respectively. The feature at 7.3  in the D ( r ) plot is related to a Zn � N3 distance, where N3 is the third nearest N atom to a given Zn 2 + ion, suggesting continuous networks of Zn-Im-Zn. The result implied the near-identical short-range order of ZIF-62 glass with its crystalline structure. FTIR (Figure S3) and Raman (Figure S4) spectra of the crystal and glass showed almost the same frequencies of intramolecular vibrations, indicating the integrity of organic linkers preserved during the melt-quenching process. Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) was applied for mapping the pore size distribution of ZIF-62 glass. [13] The pore size radius of ZIF-62 glass (3.16 ) was much larger than that of crystalline ZIF-62 (2.66 ). The result was in line with the previous report. [12]
texts.text: structure factors, provided the atom-atom distance and relational bond information. The PDFs of ZIF-62 crystal and glass were near identical for distances up to 8 . In detail, the D ( r ) curves displayed the expected peaks at interatomic distances of 1.5 , 2 , 3 , 4 , and 6 , corresponding to C � C/C � N, Zn � N1, Zn � C, Zn � N2, and Zn � Zn pair correlations, respectively. The feature at 7.3  in the D ( r ) plot is related to a Zn � N3 distance, where N3 is the third nearest N atom to a given Zn 2 + ion, suggesting continuous networks of Zn-Im-Zn. The result implied the near-identical short-range order of ZIF-62 glass with its crystalline structure. FTIR (Figure S3) and Raman (Figure S4) spectra of the crystal and glass showed almost the same frequencies of intramolecular vibrations, indicating the integrity of organic linkers preserved during the melt-quenching process. Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) was applied for mapping the pore size distribution of ZIF-62 glass. [13] The pore size radius of ZIF-62 glass (3.16 ) was much larger than that of crystalline ZIF-62 (2.66 ). The result was in line with the previous report. [12]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ZIF-62 glass still remained microporosity, as shown by the adsorption isotherms of ZIF-62 glass for CO2, CH4, and N2 (Figure S5). At 293 K and 1 bar, the gas uptakes by ZIF-62 glass (in cm 3 g � 1 ) were 11 (CO2), 2.6 (CH4), and 0.7 (N2), respectively, much lower than the gas uptakes of the crystalline precursor of 18.5 (CO2), 10 (CH4) and 2.4 (N2). The phenomenon might be attributed to the contraction of the overall volume after vitrification. Most interestingly, the reduction in the adsorption capacity for CO2 was much weaker than for CH4 and N2, demonstrating much stronger affinity between CO2 molecules and the glass. At zero CO2 uptake, the isosteric heat of adsorption (Qst 0 , kJmol � 1 ) was calculated to be 29 (ZIF-62 glass) and 26 (crystalline ZIF-62), respectively. The interaction between ZIF-62 and CO2 is enhanced after conversion into the glassy state. High IAST (ideal adsorbed solution theory) selectivities for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 binary mixtures (Figure S6) demonstrated that ZIF62 glass is a promising material for CO 2 capture.
texts.text: ZIF-62 glass still remained microporosity, as shown by the adsorption isotherms of ZIF-62 glass for CO2, CH4, and N2 (Figure S5). At 293 K and 1 bar, the gas uptakes by ZIF-62 glass (in cm 3 g � 1 ) were 11 (CO2), 2.6 (CH4), and 0.7 (N2), respectively, much lower than the gas uptakes of the crystalline precursor of 18.5 (CO2), 10 (CH4) and 2.4 (N2). The phenomenon might be attributed to the contraction of the overall volume after vitrification. Most interestingly, the reduction in the adsorption capacity for CO2 was much weaker than for CH4 and N2, demonstrating much stronger affinity between CO2 molecules and the glass. At zero CO2 uptake, the isosteric heat of adsorption (Qst 0 , kJmol � 1 ) was calculated to be 29 (ZIF-62 glass) and 26 (crystalline ZIF-62), respectively. The interaction between ZIF-62 and CO2 is enhanced after conversion into the glassy state. High IAST (ideal adsorbed solution theory) selectivities for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 binary mixtures (Figure S6) demonstrated that ZIF62 glass is a promising material for CO 2 capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A composite MOF glass membrane was fabricated by melt-quenching treatment of an in situ solvothermally synthesized polycrystalline MOF membrane on a porous ceramic alumina support (Figure 2a). With respect to the easy oxidization of organic linkers in ZIF-62 crystals at high temperature, various parameters such as temperature program and the inert atmosphere in the tubular furnace were systematically investigated to optimize the melt-quenching procedure. Herein, the melting temperature was set to 440 8 C and held for 15 min to guarantee the complete melting of the ZIF-62 solid to liquid. When it was cooled down to the ambient temperature, the ZIF-62 liquid transmitted into the glassy state, resulting in the ZIF-62 glass membrane. It should be mentioned that the melting liquid has a high viscosity and is difficult to spread evenly. Therefore, the alumina support was placed upside down in order to promote the spreading of the MOF melts by its weight. Simultaneously, the melting liquid could penetrate into the nanopores of the alumina support via capillary extrusion.
texts.text: A composite MOF glass membrane was fabricated by melt-quenching treatment of an in situ solvothermally synthesized polycrystalline MOF membrane on a porous ceramic alumina support (Figure 2a). With respect to the easy oxidization of organic linkers in ZIF-62 crystals at high temperature, various parameters such as temperature program and the inert atmosphere in the tubular furnace were systematically investigated to optimize the melt-quenching procedure. Herein, the melting temperature was set to 440 8 C and held for 15 min to guarantee the complete melting of the ZIF-62 solid to liquid. When it was cooled down to the ambient temperature, the ZIF-62 liquid transmitted into the glassy state, resulting in the ZIF-62 glass membrane. It should be mentioned that the melting liquid has a high viscosity and is difficult to spread evenly. Therefore, the alumina support was placed upside down in order to promote the spreading of the MOF melts by its weight. Simultaneously, the melting liquid could penetrate into the nanopores of the alumina support via capillary extrusion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: slow gas permeability, of H2/N2, H2/CH4, CO2/N2, and CO2/ CH4 gas pairs reached 53, 59, 23, and 26 at 25 8 C, respectively. These values were far above the corresponding Knudsen selectivities (3.7, 2.8, 0.8, and 0.6), determined by the inverse of the square-root of the molecular masses. The temperature dependency (25-200 8 C) of single-gas (H 2 , CH4, CO2, and N2) permeation characteristics was investigated (Figure S10). The permeances of H2, CH4, and N2 increased with increasing temperature (Table S2), showing activated permeation with an apparent activation energy of 4.0, 6.1, and 6.8 kJmol � 1 , respectively (Figure S11). Since the ZIF-62 glass rarely adsorbs these three gases, the high H 2 /N2 and H2/CH4 ideal selectivities are strongly related to the molecular sieving mechanism. The activation energy for permeation increased with increasing size of the permeating gases, suggesting the high quality of the glass membrane. Conversely, the CO2 permeance decreased with increasing temperature, with an apparent activation energy of � 2.6 kJmol � 1 . Since the activation energy of permeation is the sum of the heat of adsorption and the activation energy of diffusion, the negative activation energy of CO2 permeation herein should be ascribed to the high heat of adsorption for CO2 in ZIF-62 glass.

These values were far above the corresponding Knudsen selectivities (3.7, 2.8, 0.8, and 0.6), determined by the inverse of the square-root of the molecular masses. The temperature dependency (25-200 8 C) of single-gas (H 2 , CH4, CO2, and N2) permeation characteristics was investigated (Figure S10). The permeances of H2, CH4, and N2 increased with increasing temperature (Table S2), showing activated permeation with an apparent activation energy of 4.0, 6.1, and 6.8 kJmol � 1 , respectively (Figure S11). Since the ZIF-62 glass rarely adsorbs these three gases, the high H 2 /N2 and H2/CH4 ideal selectivities are strongly related to the molecular sieving mechanism. The activation energy for permeation increased with increasing size of the permeating gases, suggesting the high quality of the glass membrane. Conversely, the CO2 permeance decreased with increasing temperature, with an apparent activation energy of � 2.6 kJmol � 1 . Since the activation energy of permeation is the sum of the heat of adsorption and the activation energy of diffusion, the negative activation energy of CO2 permeation herein should be ascribed to the high heat of adsorption for CO2 in ZIF-62 glass.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The glass MOF membranes were further tested with binary gas mixtures (H2/CH4, CO2/N2, CO2/CH4). The selectivity for equimolar H2/CH4 mixture was 50.7, a result consistent with the ideal gas selectivity. Gas permeance and separation factor as a function of pressure were studied (Figure S12). The H2 permeance gradually decreased while the CH4 permeance remained basically unchanged with increasing pressure, and the selectivity value decreased from 50.7 to 33.5. The excellent performance far exceeded the corresponding Robeson upper bound (Figure 3b). Zeolite and carbon membranes are currently two types of membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MOFglass has a rather strong interaction with the substrate, which is highly beneficial to the membrane stability. The porous alumina support with about 30% porosity has a double-layered structure consisting of a coarse a -Al2 O3 layer (micropore) coated with a finer top layer of Al2O3 (nanopore) with an overall thickness of approximately 20-30 m m. The top layer could be easily filled up with the MOF melt due to the capillary force as shown in Figure 2e. The XRD pattern showed almost no peaks except for the substrate, demonstrating the successful vitrification of the ZIF-62 crystals into the glassy state.
texts.text: MOFglass has a rather strong interaction with the substrate, which is highly beneficial to the membrane stability. The porous alumina support with about 30% porosity has a double-layered structure consisting of a coarse a -Al2 O3 layer (micropore) coated with a finer top layer of Al2O3 (nanopore) with an overall thickness of approximately 20-30 m m. The top layer could be easily filled up with the MOF melt due to the capillary force as shown in Figure 2e. The XRD pattern showed almost no peaks except for the substrate, demonstrating the successful vitrification of the ZIF-62 crystals into the glassy state.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The intrinsic gas permeation properties and gas separation performance of the prepared glass membranes were evaluated by the Wicke-Kallenbach method. In contrast to the leaky polycrystalline ZIF-62 membranes, the glass membranes showed excellent gas separation performance. Figure 3a shows the single-gas permeance as a function of the molecular kinetic diameter. It was observed that the average pore diameter of the glass membrane is between the molecular kinetic diameters of CO2 and N2. The ideal selectivities, defined as the ratio of the fast to

texts.label: text
texts.orig: showing high H2/CH4 separation performance. Nevertheless, carbon membranes suffer from physical aging and zeolite membranes are beset by low reproducibility and grain boundary defects. The glass membranes with comparable performance are free of the above-mentioned shortcomings. The H2/CH4 separation performance of the glass MOF membrane exceeded that of most pure polycrystalline MOF membranes reported so far. It is generally thought that the flexibility of MOFs compromises their molecular sieving abilities.
texts.text: showing high H2/CH4 separation performance. Nevertheless, carbon membranes suffer from physical aging and zeolite membranes are beset by low reproducibility and grain boundary defects. The glass membranes with comparable performance are free of the above-mentioned shortcomings. The H2/CH4 separation performance of the glass MOF membrane exceeded that of most pure polycrystalline MOF membranes reported so far. It is generally thought that the flexibility of MOFs compromises their molecular sieving abilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separation are the core tasks in flue gas treatment and natural gas sweetening. [14] For the glass MOF membrane, the CO2 separation performance is controlled by both the molecular sieving effect and attractive interactions. The selectivities for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 are 34.5 and 36.6, respectively, with a CO2 permeability of 2602 Barrer (Table S4). The preferential adsorption of CO2 over N2 and CH4 led to improved mixed gas selectivities compared with the ideal selectivities. Both the CO 2 /N2 and CO2/CH4 separation performance exceeded the corresponding Robeson upper bound [15] and reached the industrial desired performance zone. [16] Figures S13 and S14 show the pressure dependence of mixed gas permeance for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2, respectively. When the feed pressure was increased, the CO2 permeance decreased because of the decreased ratio of the amount of adsorbed CO2 to the CO2 partial pressure (Langmuir type adsorption), which is the driving force and appears in the denominator in permeance calculations. [17] The CH4 and N2 permeances remained constant due to the basically linear adsorption isotherms of CH 4 and N2 on the ZIF-62 glass. Thus, the CO 2 /N2 and CO2/CH4 selectivities displayed a slow decrease. The ZIF-62 glass membrane is a potential candidate for CO2 removal, as proved by its high CO2 permeability as well as selectivity.
texts.text: CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 separation are the core tasks in flue gas treatment and natural gas sweetening. [14] For the glass MOF membrane, the CO2 separation performance is controlled by both the molecular sieving effect and attractive interactions. The selectivities for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 are 34.5 and 36.6, respectively, with a CO2 permeability of 2602 Barrer (Table S4). The preferential adsorption of CO2 over N2 and CH4 led to improved mixed gas selectivities compared with the ideal selectivities. Both the CO 2 /N2 and CO2/CH4 separation performance exceeded the corresponding Robeson upper bound [15] and reached the industrial desired performance zone. [16] Figures S13 and S14 show the pressure dependence of mixed gas permeance for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2, respectively. When the feed pressure was increased, the CO2 permeance decreased because of the decreased ratio of the amount of adsorbed CO2 to the CO2 partial pressure (Langmuir type adsorption), which is the driving force and appears in the denominator in permeance calculations. [17] The CH4 and N2 permeances remained constant due to the basically linear adsorption isotherms of CH 4 and N2 on the ZIF-62 glass. Thus, the CO 2 /N2 and CO2/CH4 selectivities displayed a slow decrease. The ZIF-62 glass membrane is a potential candidate for CO2 removal, as proved by its high CO2 permeability as well as selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The long-term stability of the glass membrane was further examined. Stability studies were conducted with CO2/N2 separation (Figure S15). The performance remained basically unchanged during a 48 hour test. Since the water stability of the ZIF-62 glass material was evidenced in studies of its CO 2 and N2 adsorption capacity (Figure S16), we introduced 4 mol% steam into the feed gas to study the water stability of the glass membrane (Figure S17). Both the CO2 and N2 permeances decreased gradually, whereas the CO2/N2 selectivity remained almost constant. The result was speculated to be caused by the blockage of the micropores via water adsorption. After 24 hours, the water vapor almost completely filled the micropores of the glass membrane and seriously hindered the passage of both CO 2 and N2 molecules, resulting in extremely low CO 2 and N2 permeances. Then, the membrane was in situ activated to remove the condensed water in the pores at 180 8 C for 2 h with dry feeding gas and He sweep gas. As expected, the membrane performance could basically be recovered to the original value, suggesting the good water stability of the ZIF-62 glass membrane.
texts.text: The long-term stability of the glass membrane was further examined. Stability studies were conducted with CO2/N2 separation (Figure S15). The performance remained basically unchanged during a 48 hour test. Since the water stability of the ZIF-62 glass material was evidenced in studies of its CO 2 and N2 adsorption capacity (Figure S16), we introduced 4 mol% steam into the feed gas to study the water stability of the glass membrane (Figure S17). Both the CO2 and N2 permeances decreased gradually, whereas the CO2/N2 selectivity remained almost constant. The result was speculated to be caused by the blockage of the micropores via water adsorption. After 24 hours, the water vapor almost completely filled the micropores of the glass membrane and seriously hindered the passage of both CO 2 and N2 molecules, resulting in extremely low CO 2 and N2 permeances. Then, the membrane was in situ activated to remove the condensed water in the pores at 180 8 C for 2 h with dry feeding gas and He sweep gas. As expected, the membrane performance could basically be recovered to the original value, suggesting the good water stability of the ZIF-62 glass membrane.

texts.label: text

Obviously, a dense glass layer was formed on the surface with strong adhesion. Different from hydrophilic alumina, the MOF glass and glass

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In conclusion, MOF glasses are a newly developed family of MOF materials, and their intrinsic properties and application potentials should be explored. [21] Herein, we selected ZIF-62 as a representative and fabricated a continuous MOF glass membrane for gas separation. Through the meltquenching process, a polycrystalline membrane fully converted into a glass membrane that is isotropic and free of grain boundaries. The resultant membrane exhibited high separation performance for H2/CH4, CO2/N2, and CO2/CH4 mixtures. The glass membrane also exhibited superior longterm stability, which would be very beneficial to practical applications. This study demonstrated that MOF glass could become promising candidates for constructing high-performance gas separation membranes. Furthermore, we are working on the fabrication of novel MOF glass hollow fiber membranes by utilizing their easy processability and very high thermal stability for large-scale applications.
texts.text: In conclusion, MOF glasses are a newly developed family of MOF materials, and their intrinsic properties and application potentials should be explored. [21] Herein, we selected ZIF-62 as a representative and fabricated a continuous MOF glass membrane for gas separation. Through the meltquenching process, a polycrystalline membrane fully converted into a glass membrane that is isotropic and free of grain boundaries. The resultant membrane exhibited high separation performance for H2/CH4, CO2/N2, and CO2/CH4 mixtures. The glass membrane also exhibited superior longterm stability, which would be very beneficial to practical applications. This study demonstrated that MOF glass could become promising candidates for constructing high-performance gas separation membranes. Furthermore, we are working on the fabrication of novel MOF glass hollow fiber membranes by utilizing their easy processability and very high thermal stability for large-scale applications.
